inputs.target_text:
What almost killed Wikipedia in 2005?
Explosive growth flooded the site, edits poured in.
Anyone could edit, which was brilliant and risky.
Vandalism and hoaxes spread fast, unnoticed at first.
Then the Seigenthaler hoax hit the headlines.
A respected journalist was falsely accused, reputation was damaged.
Mainstream media criticized Wikipedia's reliability and public trust fell.
Volunteer editors burned out and many left.
Edit wars made pages unstable, facts flipped back and forth.
Legal worries and reputation risk multiplied, pressure grew,
Leadership faced a crisis and they acted quickly.
They added page protection and emergency rules,
Admin tools improved and vandals were caught faster,
Sourcing rules tightened, citations became vital,
Community governance matured with votes and arbitration.
Transparency and fundraising rebuilt some public trust.
Journalists and scholars began checking entries again,
Openness stayed but guardrails were added.
Wikipedia survived by learning and changing its rules,
Today it is cautious and still a global knowledge hub."
inputs.ref_text: It's didn't work in India in 2008. Directions were unusable, as some streets didn't have names. But street names were the foundation of Google Maps, two researchers flew to India to figure it out. They asked businesses for directions, got people to sketch maps, and even followed strangers to see how they navigated unfamiliar places. They discovered that people use landmarks, not street names. Navigation involved four critical elements. Orientation, head towards the water. Description of a turn. Turn just past the big bazaar. Confirmation of the right path. You'll see a petrol station on the right. Error correction. If you reach the roundabout, you've gone too far. With these four elements, Google successfully localized maps and took full advantage of landmarks. That's why today your directions in India often say "turn left after the temple" instead of just a street name.
